comments.text: Bohman, Frieze, and Lubetzky prove in Random Triangle Removal (2012) that with high probability the final number of edges in random triangle removal is equal to $n^{3/2+o(1)}$, confirming an exponent of $3/2$ conjectured by Bollobás and Erdős. According to To Prove and Conjecture: Paul Erdős and His Mathematics (1998) pages 231-232, Bollobás and Erdős came up with the problem "at the 'Quo Vadis, Graph Theory?' conference in Fairbanks, Alaska, in 1990, while admiring the playful bears...." I've uploaded a copy of this article here for those without institutional access. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
